questions.question: What is middleware in ASP.NET Core and how does the request pipeline work?
questions.answer:
Middleware is software that's assembled into an application pipeline to handle requests and responses. Each middleware component can: process the request, pass it to the next component, process the response on the way back.

The pipeline is a chain of middleware — each has a reference to the next. Think of it as a series of delegates where each can short-circuit (return early) or continue by calling next().

// Middleware execution order (declared order matters)
app.UseExceptionHandler();   // 1st: wraps everything, catches exceptions
app.UseHsts();               // 2nd
app.UseHttpsRedirection();   // 3rd
app.UseStaticFiles();        // 4th: serves files, short-circuits if found
app.UseRouting();            // 5th: matches URL to endpoint
app.UseCors();               // 6th: CORS must be BEFORE auth
app.UseAuthentication();     // 7th: sets HttpContext.User
app.UseAuthorization();      // 8th: checks policies/roles
app.UseRateLimiter();        // 9th
app.MapControllers();        // endpoint: the actual controller actions

Request flow: Request enters at top (ExceptionHandler), passes through each middleware, reaches the controller action, then the response travels back UP through the same middleware chain in reverse.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Use(), Run(), and Map()?
followUps.answer: app.Use(async (ctx, next) => { ... await next(); ... }) — adds middleware that calls the next component. Can run code both before (on the way in) and after (on the way out) the next component. app.Run(async ctx => { ... }) — terminal middleware. Never calls next. Short-circuits the pipeline. app.Map("/api", branch => { ... }) — creates a pipeline branch that only activates for requests matching the path prefix. The branch has its own independent middleware chain. Use app.Use for most middleware, app.Run only at the very end of a pipeline, and app.Map for path-based branching.

followUps.question: What happens if middleware doesn't call next()?
followUps.answer: If middleware returns without calling next(), the pipeline is short-circuited — no downstream middleware or endpoint executes. The response is whatever the current middleware wrote to HttpContext.Response. This is called a terminal response. Examples where this is intentional: UseStaticFiles returns the file and stops; UseAuthorization returns 401/403 and stops if access is denied; custom rate-limiting middleware returns 429 and stops when limit exceeded. If you forget to call next() in a pass-through middleware, requests will appear to hang or return empty responses.

questions.tip: Order matters critically. CORS before Auth (CORS preflight must not require authentication). ExceptionHandler first (wraps everything). Routing before Auth (auth needs to know which endpoint was matched for endpoint-level authorization policies).

questions.question: How do you create custom middleware in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Two ways: convention-based class or IMiddleware interface (supports DI):

Convention-based (most common):

public class RequestLoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> _logger;

    public RequestLoggingMiddleware(
        RequestDelegate next,
        ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> logger)
    {
        _next = next;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("{Method} {Path} started",
            context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);

        await _next(context);  // call next middleware

        _logger.LogInformation("{Method} {Path} → {Status}",
            context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path,
            context.Response.StatusCode);
    }
}

// Extension method for clean registration
public static class RequestLoggingMiddlewareExtensions
{
    public static IApplicationBuilder UseRequestLogging(
        this IApplicationBuilder app)
        => app.UseMiddleware<RequestLoggingMiddleware>();
}

// Program.cs
app.UseRequestLogging();

IMiddleware interface — activated per-request from DI container. Better when the middleware needs scoped services (DbContext, etc.) because it's re-instantiated per request.

followUps.question: When would you use IMiddleware over convention-based middleware?
followUps.answer: Use IMiddleware when your middleware needs Scoped services (like DbContext or the current user). Convention-based middleware is instantiated once at startup as a Singleton — its constructor-injected services are also Singleton. If you inject a Scoped service into convention-based middleware, you get a captive dependency. IMiddleware is activated per request by the DI container, so it can receive Scoped services safely. Register the middleware with services.AddScoped<MyMiddleware>() then app.UseMiddleware<MyMiddleware>().

followUps.question: How do you short-circuit the pipeline?
followUps.answer: Simply don't call await _next(context). Write your response directly to context.Response and return. For clean short-circuiting: set context.Response.StatusCode = 429, write a body if needed, then return without calling next. This is exactly how UseStaticFiles works — if a matching file is found, it writes the file content and returns, never calling next. If no file matches, it calls next and the request proceeds to routing and controllers. The key is that once you start writing to the response body, you cannot call next (headers are already sent).

questions.tip: Short-circuit = don't call _next. If you return early without calling await _next(context), no downstream middleware executes. This is how UseStaticFiles works — if a file is found, it responds directly and never reaches your controllers.

questions.question: What is the difference between Use(), Run(), and Map() in ASP.NET Core middleware?
questions.answer:
Three methods for adding middleware components:

Use() — adds middleware that can call the next component. Most common.

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    // before
    await next();   // calls next middleware
    // after (response flowing back)
});

Run() — terminal middleware. Does NOT call next — short-circuits the pipeline. Used at the end.

app.Run(async context =>
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Hello from terminal middleware!");
    // No next() — pipeline ends here
});

Map() — branches the pipeline based on URL path. Each branch has its own middleware chain.

// All requests to /api/** go through ApiMiddleware only
app.Map("/api", apiApp =>
{
    apiApp.UseAuthentication();
    apiApp.UseAuthorization();
    apiApp.MapControllers();
});

// Requests NOT matching /api go here
app.Run(async context => await context.Response.WriteAsync("Not found"));

followUps.question: What is MapWhen()?
followUps.answer: app.MapWhen(predicate, branch) branches the pipeline based on any condition on the HttpContext — not just path prefix. Example: app.MapWhen(ctx => ctx.Request.Headers.ContainsKey("X-Api-Key"), branch => { branch.UseMiddleware<ApiKeyAuth>(); branch.MapControllers(); }). Requests matching the predicate go into the branch permanently — they don't return to the main pipeline. For conditional middleware that should rejoin the main pipeline, use UseWhen instead.

followUps.question: What is UseWhen()?
followUps.answer: app.UseWhen(predicate, branch) conditionally inserts middleware into the pipeline based on a predicate. Unlike MapWhen, requests that go through the UseWhen branch rejoin the main pipeline afterward. Example: app.UseWhen(ctx => ctx.Request.Path.StartsWithSegments("/api"), branch => branch.UseMiddleware<ApiRequestLogger>()) — API requests get the logger, then all requests continue through the rest of the pipeline (rate limiting, auth, controllers). Use UseWhen for conditional middleware that adds behavior without permanently forking the pipeline.

questions.tip: MapWhen vs UseWhen: MapWhen branches permanently (matched requests never return to the main pipeline). UseWhen conditionally adds middleware but rejoins the main pipeline after. Use MapWhen for separate sub-pipelines, UseWhen for conditional middleware injection.

questions.question: What are action filters in ASP.NET Core and when do you use them?
questions.answer:
Filters run at specific points in the MVC filter pipeline — after routing has selected the action but before/after the action executes. They're MVC-aware (have access to controller context, action descriptor, model state).

Filter types and execution order:

  1. Authorization filters — first; short-circuit if unauthorized. [Authorize]
  2. Resource filters — before model binding; good for caching.
  3. Action filters — before/after action executes. Most commonly customized.
  4. Exception filters — handle unhandled exceptions from actions.
  5. Result filters — before/after action result (IActionResult) executes.
public class ValidateModelFilter : IActionFilter
{
    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        if (!context.ModelState.IsValid)
        {
            context.Result = new BadRequestObjectResult(
                new ValidationProblemDetails(context.ModelState));
        }
    }

    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext context) { }
}

Apply globally: builder.Services.AddControllers(o => o.Filters.Add<ValidateModelFilter>());
Per-controller or action: [ServiceFilter(typeof(ValidateModelFilter))]

followUps.question: What is the difference between middleware and action filters?
followUps.answer: Middleware operates at the HTTP pipeline level — it sees raw HttpContext and has no knowledge of MVC (no controllers, no model state, no action descriptors). It runs for every request including static files and non-MVC endpoints. Action filters run inside the MVC pipeline — they have access to ActionDescriptor, ModelState, ActionArguments, and the controller instance. Use middleware for HTTP-level cross-cutting concerns (logging, CORS, rate limiting). Use filters for MVC-aware logic (model validation, response shaping, caching based on action metadata).

followUps.question: What is a global filter?
followUps.answer: A global filter applies to all controller actions in the application without needing to decorate each controller or action. Register it in builder.Services.AddControllers(options => options.Filters.Add<MyFilter>()) or options.Filters.Add(new MyFilter()). Global filters run for every MVC action — useful for cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, execution timing, or global model validation. They execute in order: global filters → controller-level filters → action-level filters. Services-registered filters (ServiceFilter) support DI; instance-registered filters don't.

questions.tip: Use filters over middleware when you need MVC context (ModelState, controller/action names, route data). Use middleware for cross-cutting concerns that operate on raw HTTP (request logging, CORS, rate limiting).

questions.question: How do you implement global exception handling with middleware?
questions.answer:
Never let unhandled exceptions return raw 500 HTML to API clients. The best approach in .NET 8 is the IExceptionHandler interface:

// .NET 8: IExceptionHandler
public class GlobalExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public GlobalExceptionHandler(ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context,
        Exception exception,
        CancellationToken ct)
    {
        _logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception");

        var (statusCode, title) = exception switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException     => (404, "Not Found"),
            UnauthorizedAccessException => (403, "Forbidden"),
            ValidationException      => (400, "Validation Error"),
            _                        => (500, "Internal Server Error")
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = statusCode,
            Title = title,
            // Don't expose exception.Message for 5xx in production
            Detail = statusCode < 500 ? exception.Message : null
        }, ct);

        return true;  // exception handled
    }
}

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<GlobalExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
app.UseExceptionHandler();

followUps.question: What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?
followUps.answer: ProblemDetails is a standardized JSON error response format defined in RFC 7807. It has well-defined fields: type (URI identifying the error type), title (human-readable summary), status (HTTP status code), detail (specific explanation), instance (URI of the specific occurrence). ASP.NET Core returns ProblemDetails automatically for 4xx responses when using [ApiController] and builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(). Standardizing on ProblemDetails means clients always get a predictable error shape — no parsing guesswork. Use ValidationProblemDetails (extends ProblemDetails) for model validation errors.

followUps.question: How do you chain multiple exception handlers?
followUps.answer: Register multiple IExceptionHandler implementations in DI order. The UseExceptionHandler middleware calls each handler's TryHandleAsync in registration order — if a handler returns true, the chain stops. If it returns false, the next handler is tried. Example: register ValidationExceptionHandler first (handles 400s), then NotFoundExceptionHandler (handles 404s), then FallbackExceptionHandler (handles everything else as 500). This is cleaner than a massive switch statement in a single handler — each handler owns one type of exception.

questions.tip: Don't return stack traces in production. Log the full exception server-side (Serilog → Azure Application Insights), return only a generic ProblemDetails to the client. Returning stack traces is a security vulnerability — attackers use them to learn your server internals.

questions.question: What is the difference between ConfigureServices and Configure in ASP.NET Core? What replaced them in .NET 6?
questions.answer:
This question tests knowledge of the ASP.NET Core startup model — both the classic pattern (pre-.NET 6) and the modern minimal hosting model (.NET 6+).

Classic pattern (ASP.NET Core 2.x–5.x) — two-class approach:

// Program.cs — creates the host
public class Program
{
    public static void Main(string[] args) =>
        CreateHostBuilder(args).Build().Run();

    public static IHostBuilder CreateHostBuilder(string[] args) =>
        Host.CreateDefaultBuilder(args)
            .ConfigureWebHostDefaults(web => web.UseStartup<Startup>());
}

// Startup.cs — two separate methods
public class Startup
{
    // ConfigureServices — runs FIRST
    // Registers services into the DI container
    // Called at build time, before the app starts handling requests
    public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services)
    {
        services.AddControllers();
        services.AddScoped<IQuestionService, QuestionService>();
        services.AddDbContext<ApplicationDbContext>(opts =>
            opts.UseSqlite(Configuration.GetConnectionString("Default")));
        services.AddAuthentication(...);
    }

    // Configure — runs SECOND
    // Builds the HTTP request pipeline (middleware order)
    // Called after the host is built, wires up middleware
    public void Configure(IApplicationBuilder app, IWebHostEnvironment env)
    {
        if (env.IsDevelopment()) app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
        app.UseHttpsRedirection();
        app.UseRouting();
        app.UseAuthentication();
        app.UseAuthorization();
        app.UseEndpoints(endpoints => endpoints.MapControllers());
    }
}

.NET 6+ minimal hosting model — single Program.cs:

// Program.cs — all in one, no Startup class needed
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

// === Replaces ConfigureServices ===
builder.Services.AddControllers();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IQuestionService, QuestionService>();
builder.Services.AddSession();

var app = builder.Build();

// === Replaces Configure ===
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
app.UseStaticFiles();
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();
app.MapControllers();

app.Run();

InterviewPrepPortal uses the .NET 6+ minimal hosting model — one Program.cs, no Startup class.

followUps.question: Can you still use the Startup class in .NET 6+?
followUps.answer: Yes — the Startup class is still supported in .NET 6+ for backward compatibility. You can call builder.WebHost.UseStartup<Startup>() to use the classic pattern. This is useful when migrating existing .NET 5 projects to .NET 6 — you don't have to rewrite everything at once. The minimal hosting model and the Startup class are functionally equivalent; both ultimately do the same thing. New projects should use the minimal hosting model (single Program.cs) — it's simpler, less boilerplate, and the officially recommended approach. One consideration: if your Startup class is very large, it may be worth keeping it for organisation, but it can also be split into extension methods on IServiceCollection and IApplicationBuilder: builder.Services.AddApplicationServices(); app.UseApplicationMiddleware();

followUps.question: What is the order of operations when an ASP.NET Core app starts?
followUps.answer: Understanding startup order helps debug "service not found" and "middleware not configured" errors: (1) WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args) — creates the builder, loads configuration (appsettings.json, environment variables, command-line args), sets up default logging. (2) All builder.Services.Add*() calls — registers services into the DI container. Services are NOT yet resolvable at this point. (3) builder.Build() — finalises the DI container (no more service registrations after this). Creates the IHost. Validates scoped services (if enabled). (4) All app.Use*() and app.Map*() calls — registers middleware components into the pipeline. (5) app.Run() — starts listening for HTTP requests. Middleware executes in registered order from this point. Important: database migrations (db.Database.Migrate()) typically happen between steps 3 and 5 by resolving a scoped service: using var scope = app.Services.CreateScope(); scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>().Database.Migrate();

questions.tip: Key difference: ConfigureServices / builder.Services = register services (DI container setup). Configure / app.Use*() = configure the middleware pipeline. For .NET 6+: everything in one Program.cs, no Startup class. The split into builder.Services.* (before Build()) and app.* (after Build()) preserves the same logical separation.
